Memory Hook: "Text me the default"

dbutils.widgets.text("department", "all", "Department")

The rule: Streaming DataFrames don't support operations that require ordering across the entire dataset — because the data is infinite and never "done."

A — simple SELECT ✅ fine
B — GROUP BY with count ✅ fine (supported via windowing/watermarks)
C — adding a column ✅ fine
D — WHERE filter ✅ fine
E — ORDER BY ❌ not supported — you can't sort a stream globally

The key insight you missed:
Spark is a distributed execution engine. Breakpoints (D) and variable watchers (A) are single-process debugger concepts — they don't work when your code is running across a cluster of executors. There's no single place to "pause and inspect."
Why B is the only practical answer:

df.printSchema() — shows column names and types
print(df.dtypes) — returns a list of (column, type) tuples
These run on the driver, before the distributed job fans out

Memory hook: "Spark doesn't pause — it prints"
Any exam question about debugging Spark jobs → think logging and schema inspection, not IDE-style debugging tools. Breakpoints, variable watchers, step-through debuggers = single machine concepts that don't translate to distributed systems.
Quick elimination for similar questions:

Variable watcher → ❌ single process only
Breakpoint → ❌ distributed, nothing to pause
Spark UI → ❌ good for performance/DAG, not runtime data types
Print/log schema → ✅ always works, runs on driver

This one's worth locking in because the underlying concept (distributed = no breakpoints) will show up in other forms on the exam.

You can NEVER compare NULL with =, !=, <>, >, < — always returns UNKNOWN, never TRUE

Databricks only allows one metastore per region per account. So one-per-workspace (E) isn't even possible — metastores are account-scoped, not workspace-scoped.

Use catalogs to separate business units, teams, or environments inside a regional metastore. That's what A describes — it's good practice within a region, just doesn't solve the multi-region problem.

The mental model:

INSERT OVERWRITE = "pour new data into the existing container" — the container shape stays fixed
CREATE OR REPLACE = "smash the container and build a new one from scratch" — shape is whatever the query produces

So schema replacement isn't a special operation — it's just a natural consequence of literally recreating the table definition every time.
Memory hook: Complete mode = "here's everything I know so far, overwriting what was there before" — fired on every trigger, not at the end.

Memory hook — just remember "ACU":
Append → Complete → Update
Or think of it as a spectrum of how much gets written each trigger:

Append = least (only new)
Update = middle (only changed)
Complete = most (everything)

Watch out for: Overwrite, Merge — those are batch/Delta write modes that show up as distractors specifically to trip you up. Any time you see Overwrite or Merge in a Structured Streaming question, eliminate those options immediately.
You already know what Complete mode does from the last question — you just needed the full list locked in. ACU and you're good.

Memory hook — "Delta doubles up on plural":

Data = many parquet files
Log entries = many JSON files
Every transaction = new files added to both

Think of _delta_log as a filing cabinet where each transaction drops in a new folder — you never erase or append to an old one, you just add a new one.
The pattern to eliminate wrong answers fast:

"single file" anywhere → ❌ wrong (Delta is always plural)
"log removed after commit" → ❌ wrong (that's the whole point of the log, it persists)
"log appended" → ❌ wrong (new file per transaction, not appended)

Memory hook — "JSON needs a blueprint"
You can't build a struct from a string without a blueprint. FROM_JSON = function + blueprint (schema). One argument is always incomplete.
Quick reference for JSON functions:
from_json(col, schema) — JSON string → Struct, needs schema
to_json(col) — Struct → JSON string, no schema needed
get_json_object(col, path) — extract one field from JSON string, path like $.name
json_tuple(col, *fields) — extract multiple fields by field names

Memory hook — match the phrase to the command:
"regardless of whether it exists" → CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE
"only if it doesn't exist" → CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS
"fails if it exists" → CREATE TABLE
Watch out for:

CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE IF EXISTS → invalid syntax, doesn't exist
AS SELECT → makes it CTAS, copies data, not an empty table
USING DELTA → unnecessary, Delta is the default in Databricks

Clean syntax to memorize:
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE students (
id STRING,
name STRING,
dateOfBirth DATE,
email STRING
)

Memory hook — Databricks uses MODIFY, not INSERT/DELETE
In Databricks SQL, you cannot grant INSERT and DELETE separately. MODIFY is the single privilege that covers insert, update, and delete.
Correct syntax to memorize:
GRANT MODIFY ON TABLE inventory TO tom.hanks@organization.com
The syntax order is always:
GRANT privilege ON TABLE tablename TO user
Watch out for:

GRANT INSERT, DELETE ... → invalid in Databricks, this is standard SQL thinking
GRANT MODIFY TO user ON inventory → wrong order, missing TABLE keyword
USAGE → only grants access to schemas/catalogs, not row-level operations

Quick privilege reference:
SELECT — read data
MODIFY — insert, update, delete rows
ALL PRIVILEGES — everything

Memory hook — Auto Loader has 3 signatures, all must be present:

readStream — it's always streaming, never spark.read
format("cloudFiles") — this is the Auto Loader identifier
checkpointLocation — required for fault tolerance, never optional

Correct pattern:
spark.readStream.format("cloudFiles").option("cloudFiles.format", source_format).load(raw_path).writeStream.option("checkpointLocation", checkpoint_path).table("bronze_table")
Eliminate wrong answers fast:

spark.read (no Stream) → batch, not Auto Loader
format("delta") → reading Delta, not ingesting new files
no checkpointLocation → incomplete, always required
writeStream.table("raw") → wrong direction, Bronze writes TO bronze, not raw

One line to remember:
cloudFiles in → checkpoint out → bronze table
If you don't see all three (readStream + cloudFiles + checkpointLocation) it's not Auto Loader.

Both — and they do different things:

RETURNS — declares the data type of the output (INT, STRING, DOUBLE, etc.)
RETURN — the actual expression or value being returned

sqlCREATE FUNCTION plus_one(value INTEGER)
RETURNS INTEGER
RETURN value + 1;
Memory hook: RETURNS = type, RETURN = value
Think of it like a function signature vs the function body. You need both every time.

Simple way to remember:

Data lake = storage layer
Data lakehouse = storage + table reliability + governance + analytics/ML platform

A managed Delta table is stored as Parquet data files, but it is not the same thing as a plain Parquet table.

In Databricks:

Delta table = Parquet files plus a Delta transaction log, stored in _delta_log
Parquet table = just Parquet files, without Delta Lake transaction metadata
So a managed Delta table physically uses Parquet for its data files, but logically it is a Delta Lake table.

That means it supports features plain Parquet does not, such as:

ACID transactions
MERGE, UPDATE, DELETE
time travel
schema enforcement/evolution
transaction history
streaming and batch consistency
A quick way to say it:

Delta tables are built on Parquet, but Delta is not just Parquet.

Memory hook:

Sequential = Size. Simultaneous = Scale.

For this question, the key clue is:

“all of their sequentially run queries”
“endpoint is not used by any other user”

That means the problem is not concurrency. The queries are waiting on the power of one warehouse/cluster, so you need a bigger cluster size.

So remember:

Slow single-file line of queries → make the machine stronger → increase cluster size
Many users/queries at once → add more lanes → increase max scaling range
Why A was tempting: “scaling range” sounds like it makes things faster, but in Databricks SQL warehouses it mostly helps when there are multiple concurrent queries/users. Since these are sequential, extra clusters do not help much.

Personal note you could add:

I missed this because I saw “all queries are slow” and thought “scale out.” But the clue is “sequential” and “no other users,” so this is not a concurrency issue. For latency of one query at a time, increase cluster size, not cluster count.

Memory hook:

Control plane controls. Data/results go to your storage.

For job results, think:

“My jobs, my results, my bucket.”

Databricks may orchestrate the job from the control plane, but the actual results are stored in the DBFS layer backed by customer-managed cloud storage like S3, ADLS, or Blob Storage.

How to avoid the trap:

Control Plane = UI, jobs service, notebooks metadata, orchestration
Data Plane = clusters run the work
DBFS/customer storage = job results/data are stored there
Personal note:

I picked Control Plane because jobs are managed there, but results are data/output. Databricks controls the job, but stores results in my cloud storage through DBFS.

Function / Term Reference

collect_set()
Keyword: Group unique
Definition: Aggregates rows into an array of unique values for each group.

collect_list()
Keyword: Group all
Definition: Aggregates rows into an array, keeping duplicates.

array_distinct() 
Keyword: Array dedupe Definition: 
Removes duplicate values from an existing array.

flatten() 
Keyword: Unnest arrays 
Definition: Converts an array of arrays into a single flat array.

explode() 
Keyword: Rows from array 
Definition: Converts each element of an array into its own row.

GROUP BY 
Keyword: Per entity 
Definition: Groups rows so aggregate functions calculate one result per group.

items.item_id 
Keyword: Field from structs 
Definition: Pulls the item_id field out of each struct inside the items array.

Memory hook:

Pool = parked cars, not a car factory.

A Databricks cluster pool is like a parking lot of warm, ready-to-use instances. When a cluster starts, it can grab those “parked cars” instead of waiting for new ones to be delivered.

So the main point is:

Cluster pools save startup time.

Correct answer:

C: Cluster pools allow you to save time when starting a new cluster
Why D was tempting:

“Share resources among multiple teams” sounds reasonable because multiple teams can use the same pool. But that is not the core purpose. The exam wants the primary Databricks benefit: faster cluster startup.

Quick distinctions:

Pool = keeps idle instances ready
Cluster = runs the Spark workload
Autoscaling = changes cluster size
Load balancing = distributes traffic/work, not what pools do
Access controls/quotas = better answer for team resource governance
Short hook:

Pools don’t balance. Pools don’t build. Pools pre-warm.

Step-by-step:

Start: x = 1, y = 2

if x < y:
1 < 2 is True
x = 2 * 1 = 2

if x == y:
2 == 2 is True
x += 1  -> x = 3
y -= 2  -> y = 0

return (x, y)
return (3, 0)
So y becomes 0 because it starts as 2, then this line runs once:

y -= 2
That means:

y = y - 2

Memory hook:

Array of structs needs a nickname.

Correct answer:

FILTER(students, i -> i.total_courses < 3)
Why:

students is an array
Each element inside the array is probably a struct
To inspect each student, you give each array element a temporary name, like i
Then you check the field:
i.total_courses < 3
Think of it like:

FILTER(array, each_item -> condition_on_each_item)
So:

FILTER(students, i -> i.total_courses < 3)
means:

Go through each student i in the students array and keep only students where i.total_courses < 3.

Why C was tempting:

FILTER(students, total_courses < 3)
It has the right function, but it does not say whose total_courses field to check. Spark needs the lambda variable.

Exam hook:

FILTER keeps. TRANSFORM changes. Lambda names the item.

Or shorter:

Filter an array? Name each element first.

Examples:

FILTER(students, i -> i.total_courses < 3)
TRANSFORM(students, i -> i.total_courses)
FILTER returns fewer elements.
TRANSFORM returns changed elements.

No if, no else, no parentheses. Assert condition, message.

Personal note:

I picked the English-looking version, but Python assert has its own simple grammar: condition first, message after comma.

Why:

%scala is needed for the Scala helper function.
%sql is the direct magic command to query the Delta table in SQL.
%fs is the direct magic command to list files in DBFS.
So as written, the question really points to three magics: A, B, C.

Why the imported key may have said A, C:

In a Python notebook, the SQL check could be done without %sql, for example via spark.sql(...).
But the question explicitly asks which magic commands are required for those tasks, and for a SQL query the matching magic is %sql.
So I’d mark this question as:

ambiguous / bad multi-answer question
recommended fix: change it to “Which three magic commands…”
corrected answer: A, B, C

Databricks notebook magics include %scala and %sql: Develop code in Databricks notebooks

Memory hook:

“COPY remembers what it copied.”

For Databricks:

COPY INTO = batch file loader that remembers loaded files through the Delta log.
Auto Loader / cloudFiles = streaming-style loader that needs checkpoints.
INSERT INTO = blindly pours data in again.
MERGE = matches rows by keys, not files.
Exam phrase to lock in:

If the job is batch and the question says “rerun without duplicates” or “no checkpoint,” think: COPY INTO.

I missed this because the stem led with AI and semantic understanding, so I matched it to DatabricksIQ. The real clue was “high-performance execution engine” and “accelerates SQL and DataFrame workloads,” which means Photon. DatabricksIQ = intelligence/semantic layer; Photon = execution/performance engine.

Note: 
DatabricksIQ is a valid Databricks term.

Databricks materials describe it as the Data Intelligence Engine, with wording like “Data Intelligence Engine (DatabricksIQ)” that understands the uniqueness of customer data. Source: Databricks Partner Program guide.

But there’s a nuance: in newer docs, Databricks often talks more in terms of Databricks AI assistive features, Genie, Genie Spaces, AI-generated comments, Intelligent Search, and semantic understanding rather than making DatabricksIQ the main user-facing product name. See Databricks AI assistive features and What is Databricks?.

So for the exam:

DatabricksIQ = intelligence/semantic AI layer
Photon = SQL/DataFrame performance execution engine

Your instinct that DatabricksIQ sounded like the semantic thing was right. The trick was that the question asked for the thing that is not the semantic layer.

"Mark the water BEFORE you build the window"

You check the tide (watermark) before you decide where to build your sandcastle (window).


Watermark = how long you wait for late waves (late data) — 2 hours
Window = the size of your sandcastle plot — 1 hour
Order matters: mark first, group second


So when you see a streaming + late data question, mentally say:

"withWatermark → groupBy(window) → agg"

And D is a trap — filter on current_timestamp is a batch mindset. It physically drops late rows rather than managing state. Watermark accepts late data up to the threshold, then cleanly closes state. Filter just blindly cuts data off.

Why C is wrong too — easy to mix up: the watermark duration should match the lateness tolerance (2 hrs), and the window should match the aggregation bucket (1 hr). C swaps them.

"Tables for tables, Volumes for files"

Think of Unity Catalog like a filing cabinet:

Tables = spreadsheets (structured, rows/columns)
Volumes = manila folders (raw files — PDFs, images, audio, video)



The path is the giveaway:

/Volumes/<catalog>/<schema>/<volume>/...

If you see a question mention "standard file paths" + "non-tabular" + "Unity Catalog governed" — that's the Volumes trifecta. All three boxes → C.

Why D is the trap:
DBFS mounts are the old way — pre-Unity Catalog. They're not governed by UC permissions, which the question explicitly requires. Anytime a question says "Unity Catalog permissions" + files, DBFS is automatically out.

Quick mental check:

PDFs/images/video → not a table → Volume
SQL rows/columns → Table
"DBFS mount" + "UC governed" in the same sentence → contradiction → wrong answer

A window needs three things: PARTITION, ORDER, FRAME

Think of it like a racetrack:

partitionBy = which race (each store runs its own race)
orderBy = the order runners cross the line (by date)
rowsBetween = "count everyone from the starting gun up to right now"

All three required for a true cumulative sum. Missing any one = wrong answer.

The core rule: Built-in notebook versioning = linear history only (like a simple changelog). Repos = full Git, meaning branches, merges, PRs, remote sync. Any question contrasting the two is almost certainly fishing for branching.
Memory hook: Repos vs. notebook versioning → the answer is always branches. That's the one thing notebook history fundamentally cannot do.

Auto Loader = Structured Streaming in a trench coat. It is just Structured Streaming underneath, with the cloudFiles source bolted on top to watch cloud storage. Layer / What it is: Structured Streaming = The engine | cloudFiles source = The cloud storage watcher | Auto Loader = Both together, packaged nicely. Any time a question asks "what powers Auto Loader" or "what is Auto Loader built on" the answer is always Structured Streaming. Delta Live Tables is a pipeline framework, DataFrames are batch, and Loader/Live DataFrames are not real things.

The core concept you need locked in:
In Databricks, "unmanaged" and "external" mean the same thing. You don't use the word UNMANAGED in the SQL. The way you tell Databricks it's external is simply by specifying a LOCATION. That's it.
Memory hook: External table = just add LOCATION to a normal CREATE TABLE. No special keywords. No UNMANAGED, no AS. Just:
sqlCREATE TABLE name USING DELTA LOCATION 'path'
The question uses the word "unmanaged" to tempt you toward option D. Don't take the bait — that word never appears in actual DDL syntax.

Memory hook: JDBC tables in Databricks SQL always use USING jdbc OPTIONS (...) — never jdbc() as a function. And the question asking "throws error if exists" = no IF NOT EXISTS clause. D is the trap because it looks complete but silently swallows the error.

Exam hook:

USING chooses the source. OPTIONS supplies the connection.

Or shorter:

JDBC table? USING jdbc OPTIONS. Error if exists? leave out IF NOT EXISTS.

READ THE DAMN QUESTION IN SLOW MOTION

Memory hook: "Non-matching from both" = FULL. Any time you see the word "both" describing the non-matching rows, it's FULL OUTER JOIN, not LEFT.
Memory hook: "Bronze, Silver, Gold = Step 1, Step 2, Step 3" — pure sequence, like a recipe.
Memory hook: "Many dependencies = Let Databricks manage the graph" → Declarative Pipelines. Jobs task dependencies = you're the orchestrator. DLT = Databricks is the orchestrator.

Memory hook: EXTENDED = everything the DBA cares about. Plain DESCRIBE = what a developer needs to write a query. EXTENDED = what you need to debug, audit, or manage the table.
Exam tip: if a question involves finding a table's storage location, format, or properties — the answer involves DESCRIBE EXTENDED (or DESCRIBE DETAIL for Delta-specific file-level stats).

Memory hook:

File filtering happens in the path, not the WHERE.

Correct idea:

SELECT *
FROM json./Volumes/commerce/landing/transactions/transactions_2023_*.json
Your answer E was tempting because LIKE 'transactions_2023_%' looks like filtering, but that filters rows after reading data. It does not tell Spark which files to read.

Key distinction:

Symbol Used for Example

File path wildcard transactions_2023_*.json
% SQL LIKE wildcard WHERE name LIKE 'transactions_2023_%'
Exam hook:
Star before scan. Percent after scan.

Meaning:

in the file path limits files Spark reads
% in LIKE filters values after Spark has already read data
Also, Spark SQL file path syntax should usually use backticks around the path:
SELECT *
FROM json./Volumes/commerce/landing/transactions/transactions_2023_*.json
Even if the answer choices omit backticks, answer A has the right concept: JSON format prefix + wildcard in the file path.

Memory hook: Glob * in the path = file-level filter before Spark reads anything. _metadata = after Spark opens the files. Use the glob when you can — it's cleaner and more efficient.

Memory hook: "Merge = marriage of old and new. Overwrite = divorce and remarry." New column showing up = merge, not overwrite. And it has to be in both options because the source and target both need to agree.
Memory hook: "EXPLODE makes the array blow up into rows." Any time you need to JOIN on an array column, you EXPLODE it first to get joinable individual values.
Memory hook: "DLT event log = Delta table at a path. You can't query a path, you query a view." Two steps: CREATE TEMP VIEW → then SELECT.

TBLPROPERTIES syntax depends on the command:

CREATE TABLE — inline, NO SET:
CREATE TABLE inventory (id INT, units FLOAT) TBLPROPERTIES (business_owner = 'supply_chain')

ALTER TABLE — requires SET:
ALTER TABLE inventory SET TBLPROPERTIES (business_owner = 'supply_chain')

VACUUM retention uses the same ALTER TABLE + SET pattern:
ALTER TABLE table_name SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration = 'interval 365 days')

Memory hook: "CREATE = just do it. ALTER = SET it."

Eliminate fast:
CREATE TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES → wrong (no SET on CREATE)
CREATE TABLE ... SET (...) → wrong
CREATE TABLE ... SET PROPERTY → wrong (not a real clause)
CREATE TABLE ... TBLPROPERTIES (...) → correct
ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES (...) → correct

This one's tricky because jdbc feels like the right answer but it's actually the full class path that Spark requires:

CREATE TABLE orders
USING org.apache.spark.sql.jdbc
OPTIONS (
  url "jdbc:sqlite:/bookstore.db",
  dbtable "orders"
)

The distinction:

USING jdbc — shorthand that works in DataFrame API (spark.read.format("jdbc"))
USING org.apache.spark.sql.jdbc — the fully qualified format required in Spark SQL DDL

That said — flag this one. Earlier today we established that USING jdbc is valid Spark SQL syntax, and many Databricks docs use it that way. This may be another bad answer key question. If you see it on the exam, jdbc shorthand is widely accepted.

Memory hook: "SQL DDL wants the full name. DataFrame API accepts the nickname." But honestly both show up in real Databricks code, so if the exam offers only one, pick whichever is available.

(spark.readStream
  .format("delta")
  .load("/mnt/silver_data")
  .groupBy("device_id")
  .count()
  .writeStream
  .format("delta")
  .option("checkpointLocation", "/mnt/checkpoints/gold")
  .start("/mnt/gold_data"))

Why the others fail:

A — reads from bronze, not silver
B — reads silver as JSON format, but silver is a Delta table
C — your pick — uses spark.read / write (batch), not streaming. Question specifically asks for a streaming query
E — missing checkpointLocation and uses .save() instead of .start() — both wrong for streaming


The two things that make D the only correct streaming answer:

checkpointLocation is required for streaming writes — no checkpoint = invalid streaming query
.start() not .save() — .save() is batch, .start() is streaming

Memory hook: "Streaming needs a checkpoint and a start. No checkpoint = no streaming."
Need the .db at the end
Read the question more Carefully
Read more carefully.

Think of it like:

"Stack → Smash → Deduplicate"


array_agg = stack arrays
flatten = smash them together
array_distinct = remove duplicates


Memory hook: Array of arrays across rows → array_distinct(flatten(array_agg())). Think outside-in: agg first, flatten second, distinct third.

Memory Hook

“Shrink first, count later”

dropDuplicates = shrink data
groupBy + count = cheap aggregation


dau_df = (
  orders_df
    .select("order_date", "customer_id")
    .dropDuplicates(["order_date", "customer_id"])
    .groupBy("order_date")
    .agg(F.count("*").alias("dau"))
)

✅ One-liner to remember

Auto Loader uses checkpointing to track ingestion progress—not watermarking.
Memory hook: USAGE = the key to the building — you can get in and look around, but you still need separate keys for each room (SELECT, MODIFY, etc.).
Memory hook: Serverless jobs = set it and forget it PySpark — Databricks handles the infra, you just run the job.
Memory hook: SQL Warehouse = Auto-STOP. Cluster = Auto-TERMINATE. Same idea, different name — match the term to the compute type.
Memory hook: Schema + Checkpoint = both locations, both requirements. If a question mentions schema evolution and restart recovery, you need both options present.

Memory hook: View = a window someone else cut in the wall. The owner cut it and can see through it — you just look through their window. You don't need keys to the room behind it.
The trap was "auto-masking" — that's Dynamic Data Masking, which is a separate, explicit feature you configure. It doesn't happen automatically just because someone lacks table access.
"Concurrency = scale OUT, slow queries = scale UP"
Memory hook: "What file did this row come from?" → input_file_name() — it's the only one that actually exists in Spark SQL.
